case_name: Groundwater recharge from reclaimed water with Vorlan, cyanide, and nitrobenzene under limited characterization
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for reclaimed water intended for groundwater recharge where Vorlan, cyanide, and nitrobenzene are present or suspected under incomplete site characterization and regulatory uncertainty.

input_data.scenario_background: A water reuse utility plans to divert tertiary municipal reclaimed water to managed aquifer recharge basins and possibly to injection after additional polishing. Recent screening detected low-level Vorlan, intermittent cyanide, and nitrobenzene, but the temporal pattern is unclear because samples were grab-based and industrial contribution to the sewer catchment is only partially mapped. It is also unknown whether cyanide occurs mainly as free cyanide or includes weak/strong metal-cyanide complexes, and several trace organics are suspected but not fully characterized. The utility needs a robust recommendation that can satisfy groundwater recharge expectations despite uncertain influent variability and evolving regulatory interpretation for emerging contaminants.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Produce recharge-quality water with strong control of cyanide, nitrobenzene, Vorlan, dissolved organics, particles, and pathogens, while reducing uncertainty for aquifer protection and supporting either infiltration basin or more stringent recharge implementation.
secondary_goals: provide a multi-barrier train robust to intermittent contaminant spikes
minimize risk from unknown co-occurring trace organics
maintain low fouling potential for advanced barriers
address groundwater recharge regulatory scrutiny with explainable treatment logic
allow staged monitoring and operational verification under incomplete site data

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_online_monitoring: buffer variable reclaimed water quality and provide operational warning for cyanide and organic spikes
core_unit_functions.pH_control_and_targeted_oxidation_for_cy: convert and destroy free cyanide and part of weak-acid-dissociable cyanide before downstream adsorption and membranes
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon_or_GAC_adsor: remove nitrobenzene, Vorlan, dissolved organics, and some unknown trace organics while lowering AOP demand
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: polish particles and protect RO from colloidal fouling
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide strong dissolved contaminant barrier for recharge applications and reduce salinity and many trace organics
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: provide additional destruction of residual low-molecular-weight organics and a polishing barrier for uncertain trace contaminants
core_unit_functions.final_stabilization_and_disinfection: adjust finished water chemistry and maintain microbial safety before recharge

applicability_rationale: a multi-barrier advanced train is justified because groundwater recharge has low tolerance for uncertainty and the contaminant mix includes both legacy and emerging compounds
equalization and online monitoring rank highly because the influent pattern is not well characterized and grab samples likely understate peaks
cyanide requires explicit front-end attention because speciation is unknown and untreated cyanide can interfere with biological and downstream processes
GAC or BAC is well aligned with hydrophobic pesticide removal and nitroaromatic control while also reducing unknown organic background before membranes and UV-AOP
RO plus UV-AOP offers a conservative barrier combination for recharge when regulatory interpretation is ambiguous and site data are incomplete
potential_risks: cyanide oxidation performance depends on whether significant complexed cyanide is present, so bench testing and speciation are needed
RO generates concentrate that may contain concentrated cyanide-related species and trace organics, creating a residuals management challenge
UV-AOP efficiency may be reduced by nitrate, scavengers, and background DOC if upstream organic control is inconsistent
if Vorlan and other suspected organics are more variable than indicated, GAC changeout frequency and AOP dose may be higher than expected
recommendation is robust but may exceed minimum needs if future monitoring shows contaminants are only sporadic and very low

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_online_monitoring: reduce the effect of temporal spikes and improve process control under uncertain influent composition
core_unit_functions.pH_control_and_targeted_oxidation_for_cy: lower cyanide risk before ozone and biofiltration
core_unit_functions.ozonation: transform nitrobenzene precursors and some trace organics and improve biodegradability of residual organics
core_unit_functions.biological_filtration: remove biodegradable oxidation byproducts and reduce DOC for recharge compatibility
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb Vorlan, residual nitrobenzene, and non-characterized micropollutants
core_unit_functions.ultraviolet_disinfection: provide final pathogen control for recharge application

applicability_rationale: this train avoids RO concentrate generation, which fits the site's desire to limit brine management burden
ozone followed by biologically active filtration and GAC is a recognized advanced polishing approach for reclaimed water with trace organics
for moderate TDS reclaimed water, non-desalting advanced polishing may be acceptable if recharge rules and hydrogeologic setting do not require RO-level dissolved contaminant barriers
the sequence is more compatible with medium operation complexity than a full membrane-plus-AOP train
potential_risks: regulatory ambiguity is a major caveat because some recharge programs may still prefer or require a stronger dissolved-contaminant barrier than ozone biofiltration GAC alone
ozone does not guarantee cyanide destruction if speciation and dosing are not well matched, so dedicated cyanide control remains necessary
bromide is present and creates a bromate formation risk during ozonation that must be assessed and controlled
without RO, dissolved salts and some small neutral compounds may not be reduced enough for more stringent recharge pathways
performance against unknown co-occurring micropollutants is less conservative than rank 1 under incomplete characterization

core_unit_functions.equalization: stabilize quality fluctuations and allow composite-based control decisions
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_diffused_aeration_with_: remove volatile cyanide species if free cyanide is significant while controlling air emissions
core_unit_functions.biological_treatment_polishing: reduce residual biodegradable organics and ammonia
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb Vorlan, nitrobenzene, and additional hydrophobic trace organics
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: remove particles and lower fouling potential for UV-AOP systems and final recharge stability
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: destroy residual dissolved organics not reliably captured by carbon alone
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: provide microbial polishing prior to recharge

applicability_rationale: this route can be considered if pilot data show cyanide is predominantly free cyanide and therefore amenable to volatilization or air-based pretreatment under controlled conditions
GAC and UV-AOP together give layered protection for nitrobenzene, Vorlan, and uncertain micropollutants without RO concentrate generation
the train may be attractive where salinity is acceptable for recharge and the main concern is trace organic and cyanide risk rather than TDS reduction
potential_risks: this option is highly conditional because cyanide stripping is inappropriate if cyanide is mainly complexed or if air-emission controls are not robust
air handling and worker safety become important due to possible HCN release under upset conditions
biological polishing may not tolerate cyanide excursions if front-end control is inconsistent
without RO, this remains less conservative for groundwater recharge under uncertain trace contaminant mixtures
recommendation carries a substantial uncertainty flag and should only follow speciation testing and pilot confirmation

core_unit_functions.chemical_precipitation_or_coagulation_cl: remove suspended solids and any metal-associated cyanide fraction if present
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb nitrobenzene, Vorlan, and some unknown organics
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: provide partial dissolved contaminant reduction and lower fouling risk relative to tighter RO in some cases

applicability_rationale: included as an intermediate but lower-ranked alternative when the utility seeks some membrane barrier with potentially lower pressure than RO
NF may provide meaningful reduction for some dissolved organics and multivalent constituents while using GAC to address hydrophobic pesticides
the train may be attractive if pilot testing shows cyanide is partly metal-associated and can be partially addressed by front-end clarification
potential_risks: NF is less reliable than RO for small neutral organics and dissolved contaminants, which is a significant caveat for recharge under regulatory ambiguity
chemical clarification is not a dependable stand-alone cyanide solution when speciation is unknown
concentrate management still exists, though usually less severe than RO
this train may underperform if unknown micropollutants include compounds with weak NF rejection and limited GAC affinity

core_unit_functions.sand_or_cloth_filtration: remove residual particles from tertiary effluent
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: provide some adsorption of hydrophobic organics

applicability_rationale: included as a low-ranked baseline to show that simple polishing-only trains may appear attractive for cost and footprint but are not sufficiently protective here
it could improve aesthetics and provide partial trace organic reduction, but it lacks a robust cyanide barrier and does not address uncertainty in co-occurring dissolved contaminants
potential_risks: insufficient as a stand-alone strategy for groundwater recharge given cyanide uncertainty and partial characterization of trace organics
GAC alone may not consistently remove nitrobenzene and Vorlan through breakthrough cycles without extensive monitoring
no strong dissolved contaminant barrier is present, making regulatory acceptance doubtful
does not manage possible contaminant spikes from intermittent industrial discharge

preferred_rank_pattern: multi-barrier advanced trains should rank above simple polishing trains for groundwater recharge
equalization and monitoring should be recognized as important because contaminant occurrence is intermittent and site data are limited
recommendations that explicitly address cyanide before downstream biological or membrane barriers should rank higher
solutions with stronger barriers to unknown trace organics should rank above options relying on single-process removal

must_reflect: regulatory ambiguity for groundwater recharge should push recommendations toward conservative multi-barrier treatment or clearly stated caveats
limited site data and uncertain cyanide speciation should be reflected as a design and ranking uncertainty, not ignored
co-occurring legacy and emerging contaminants require staged treatment rather than a single unit process
brine generation tradeoffs should be explicitly discussed for membrane-intensive options

must_contain: why cyanide speciation uncertainty changes process suitability and sequencing
why GAC or BAC is relevant for Vorlan and nitrobenzene control in reclaimed water polishing
why groundwater recharge generally favors conservative multi-barrier treatment under incomplete contaminant characterization
why simpler tertiary polishing alone is insufficient for this case

should_mention: unknown free versus complexed cyanide fractions and resulting treatment uncertainty
possible intermittent industrial discharge causing contaminant spikes not captured by grab samples
membrane concentrate management if RO or NF is selected
bromate formation risk if ozone is proposed due to measurable bromide in reclaimed water
breakthrough uncertainty for GAC when Vorlan and other suspected micropollutants are not fully characterized
differences in recharge regulatory acceptability between infiltration-based recharge and more stringent advanced recharge pathways
